title: Over 16 U.S. States File Lawsuit Against Trump Administration Over EV Charging Infrastructure Funding Freeze
content:
On December 16, 2025, California Attorney General Rob Bonta, alongside Governor Gavin Newsom, the California Department of Transportation (Caltrans), and the California Energy Commission (CEC), announced the filing of a lawsuit against the Trump Administration for unlawfully suspending federal funding for electric vehicle charging infrastructure. The lawsuit, co-led with a coalition of 17 state attorneys general and the State of Pennsylvania, challenges the U.S. Department of Transportationâ€™s refusal to approve new funding under two bipartisan programs established by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA).

The programs at issue are the Charging and Fueling Infrastructure (CFI) Program and the Electric Vehicle Charger Reliability and Accessibility Accelerator Program. Both are five-year initiatives created by Congress to support the construction, repair, and maintenance of EV charging infrastructure nationwide. According to the complaint, USDOT and the Federal Highway Administration have halted all new funding obligations under these programs since spring 2025, without explanation or public notice.

The plaintiffs argue that the suspension violates the constitutional separation of powers and the Administrative Procedure Act, as Congress explicitly appropriated these funds and federal agencies are required to implement duly enacted statutes. The lawsuit seeks a court declaration that the funding freeze is unlawful and an injunction preventing the Administration from continuing to withhold the funds.

California stands to lose approximately $179.8 million in federal funding as a result of the suspension. This includes grants for EV charging infrastructure along freight corridors, investments supporting zero-emission freight transportation near major ports, and funding for the repair and replacement of non-operational public EV chargers. State officials emphasized that the funding is critical to meeting Californiaâ€™s clean transportation, air quality, and climate goals.

California currently leads the nation in zero-emission vehicle adoption, with more than 2.4 million ZEVs sold to date. State officials warned that the funding pause has already delayed planned projects, disrupted infrastructure deployment, and created financial uncertainty for states and local governments.
author: Analysts Account
